problemRecovery.title: Recover The Downstream Money Flow
problemRecovery.recoveredProblem: The case is not mainly Mars versus the Moon. The load-bearing problem is that a launch-cost collapse re-prices every downstream constraint, so the value migrates to payload classes, infrastructure, inputs, and services that were previously uneconomic.
problemRecovery.hiddenConstraint: The first beneficiaries are often not the rocket company; they are the bottleneck inputs and enablers behind the new cost curve.
problemRecovery.falsifier: If Starship cadence and cost fail to approach reusable super-heavy economics, the downstream re-rating thesis weakens.

candidates.title: Payload Class Unlock
candidates.summary: Rank payload categories that become economic only after launch cost falls by an order of magnitude.
candidates.mechanism: Compare old Falcon-era payload economics with cheap-cadence economics for orbital compute, manufacturing, sensors, and servicing.
candidates.claimedDelta: Turns the thesis from destination debate into a map of newly viable markets.

candidates.title: Space Picks And Shovels
candidates.summary: Follow the money into propellant, ground systems, space-rated components, thermal materials, robotics, and test infrastructure.
candidates.mechanism: Trace every high-cadence launch into repeat consumables, bottleneck components, and operational services.
candidates.claimedDelta: Finds second-order beneficiaries before they are priced like obvious space plays.

candidates.title: Orbital Compute Loop
candidates.summary: Treat cheap launch as a possible unlock for orbital data centers and firm-power arbitrage.
candidates.mechanism: Link launch-cost collapse to deployable mass, solar power, cooling constraints, and downlink economics.
candidates.claimedDelta: Connects the launch thesis to AI infrastructure rather than only satellites.

critics.pressure: Grounded because the problem statement is explicitly about cost thresholds for payload classes.
critics.revisionMandate: Name the first payload class whose unit economics cross the line.

critics.pressure: More useful than destination debate because it creates a market map.
critics.revisionMandate: Avoid listing science-fiction payloads without near-term economics.
Attach each payload class to the launch-cost threshold that unlocks it.

critics.pressure: Strong because it uses the stated EV-input analogy and follows repeat bottlenecks.
critics.revisionMandate: Separate recurring consumables from one-time infrastructure.

critics.pressure: Best blindside: value can accrue to suppliers rather than the rocket narrative.
critics.revisionMandate: Name the least obvious bottleneck supplier class.

critics.pressure: Mechanism follows the money from launch cadence into procurement and operations.
Interesting cross-thesis link, but more speculative than picks-and-shovels.
critics.revisionMandate: Define the power, cooling, and downlink bottlenecks.

critics.pressure: Novel and memorable, but risks outrunning near-term evidence.
critics.revisionMandate: Frame it as an option unlocked by mass-to-orbit, not the main thesis.
Fuse with the payload-class threshold map.
